crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis:
Package: agent-security-harness (whl)
Version: 3.3.0
Description: 274 security tests for AI agent systems - MCP, A2A, L402, x402 wire-protocol testing, 20 enterprise platform adapters, GTG-1002 APT simulation

The agent-security-harness package is a comprehensive security testing framework designed for red-teaming AI agent deployments and wire protocols (MCP, A2A, L402, x402). The source code consists of various test harnesses that simulate adversarial attacks such as prompt injection, tool overreach, identity spoofing, and payment protocol manipulation. The logic is entirely driven by user-provided target URLs or local server commands. It uses standard Python libraries (urllib, subprocess) to interact with these targets and generate reports. No evidence of malicious behavior, unauthorized data exfiltration, or hidden payload downloads was found; the 'exfiltration' logic within the code is used to validate whether the target system under test is vulnerable to leaking information.

The package implements an AI-powered security engineering assistant (CIPHER) designed to provide expertise across offensive (RED), defensive (BLUE), and architectural (ARCHITECT) security domains, leveraging RAG and external LLMs (Claude, Ollama).

While the source code and embedded knowledge base contain extensive and highly sensitive material regarding offensive techniques (e.g., EDR evasion, reverse shells, AD exploits, cloud attacks), this content is consistent with the explicit purpose of the tool ('Security Engineering Assistant'). The application is designed to be transparent about its function and includes internal guardrails (e.g., strict authorization scope in the Signal bot, configuration redaction, and ethical constraints codified in the system prompt).

There is no evidence of unauthorized data collection, malicious persistence, or external C2 directed toward unknown endpoints. The use of the Anthropic SDK, ChromaDB, and Signalbot is legitimate based on the package description. The overall architecture is indicative of a professional security tool built for authorized testing and research.

Description: 189 security tests for AI agent systems - MCP, A2A, L402 wire-protocol testing, 20 enterprise platform adapters, GTG-1002 APT simulation

The agent-security-harness package is a comprehensive security testing framework for AI agent protocols (MCP, A2A, L402) and enterprise platforms. It functions as a red-teaming tool, allowing users to run 189 different security tests against their own agent deployments. The code logic involves generating and sending adversarial payloads (such as prompt injections, SQL injections, and sandbox escape attempts) to user-provided target URLs and analyzing the responses for vulnerabilities. While it uses subprocesses to launch local test targets and contains strings representing malicious actions, these are strictly used for testing the security of the target systems. No evidence of unauthorized data exfiltration, unexpected binary execution on the host, or malicious payload downloads was found.

There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
